schools.summary: Matthew Henry: Ephesus a city of great note, famous for the temple of Diana; Paul planted there while Apollos watered at Corinth, grudging not that Apollos built on his foundation but rejoicing (the harmony of the labourers, 1 Corinthians 3:6); Gill: Apollos at Corinth watering Paul's planting; Paul found 'certain disciples' — believers yet imperfectly instructed (having only John's baptism, not having heard of the Pentecostal gift), the providence bringing the teacher to instruct the imperfectly taught.

Barnes/Gill: Paul asks not of the ordinary converting grace (which he would not doubt they had, having believed) but of the extraordinary, miraculous gifts (tongues, prophecy) conferred on most societies in that age; the distinction (ordinary grace given to all believers, the extraordinary gifts a special endowment); the disciples' 'we have not heard whether there be any Holy Ghost' means not ignorance of the Spirit's existence but that they had not heard the Holy Ghost was yet given (the Pentecostal outpouring, John 7:39) — imperfectly instructed, needing fuller teaching.

debates.question: What does 'we have not heard whether there be any Holy Ghost' mean?

sides.position: Not ignorance of the Spirit's existence, but that they had not heard the Holy Ghost was yet given (the Pentecostal outpouring, John 7:39)

themes: have ye received the Holy Ghost since ye believed
not heard whether there be any Holy Ghost

schools.summary: Barnes: 'Unto what were ye baptized?' = unto what faith or doctrine, what did you profess when baptized; Gill: Paul takes for granted they were baptized (being disciples) and asks into what; the answer 'unto John's baptism' — they had received only the baptism of John (the baptism of repentance, pointing forward to the Messiah), not the fuller Christian baptism nor the gift of the Spirit; the imperfection of their instruction (John's baptism preparatory, pointing forward), needing to be led from John to Christ; the disciples' honest, teachable confession.

themes: unto what then were ye baptized

schools.summary: Barnes: John baptized with the baptism of repentance (a profession of repentance), his design to turn the people from sin and prepare them for the Messiah, directing their attention to him that was to come (John 1:15, 22-27); Gill: Paul shows John's baptism agreeable to and pointing to Christ; Paul leads the disciples from John to Christ — showing John himself pointed to the coming Messiah, the baptism of repentance preparatory and leading to faith in Christ; John the forerunner (not the end but the way to Christ), the wise teacher building on what they knew.

themes: John baptized with the baptism of repentance
believe on him which should come after

schools.summary: Barnes addresses the rebaptism question — some take the verse as Luke's narrative of John's converts (to avoid rebaptism), but this a most forced construction; the natural reading is that the disciples, hearing Paul, were now baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus (having had only John's baptism, now receiving Christian baptism); the distinction (John's baptism preparatory, pointing to the coming Christ; Christian baptism into the accomplished work of the risen Lord); peculiar to those who had only John's baptism (not a precedent for rebaptizing validly-baptized Christians).

sides.position: Yes — having only John's baptism, they were now baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus (the natural reading)
No — the verse is Luke's narrative of John's converts (a forced construction, rejected)

themes: baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus

schools.summary: Gill: they had been baptized (now in Christ's name), and Paul laid hands on them as Peter and John on the Samaritans (Acts 8:17), with the same effects; Clarke: they received the miraculous gift of different languages, teaching the Christian doctrines; the extraordinary gift Paul asked of (v.2, tongues and prophecy), not the ordinary regenerating grace, conferred through the apostle's hands (the apostolic prerogative, the means in that age); the completion of the disciples' instruction (from John's baptism to the fulness); peculiar to the apostolic age, not a universal pattern.

themes: Paul laid his hands upon them
the Holy Ghost came on them
they spake with tongues, and prophesied
the apostolic conferring of the gift

schools.summary: Gill: the number small (so the historian certain of it), these the first materials of a gospel church at Ephesus that became very large and flourishing (some likely becoming its elders); Ellicott suggests they were living as a kind of ascetic community, attending the Church's meetings yet not sharing the fulness of its life; about twelve disciples — a small beginning of the great Ephesian church (the great work from the small start, not despising the day of small things, Zechariah 4:10), the providence bringing them into the fulness.

themes: all the men were about twelve
the first materials of the Ephesian church
the great work from the small start

schools.summary: Chrysostom: Paul forcing his way into the synagogue in every place, wishing the occasion (for turning to the Gentiles) given by the Jews themselves, 'three months arguing and persuading concerning the kingdom of God,' provoking them to jealousy (that the Gentiles receiving the word, the Jews might repent); Paul preaching boldly yet with argument (disputing, reasoning from the Scriptures) and persuasion (urging the conscience, 2 Corinthians 5:11) — the reasoned, persuasive ministry, the kingdom of God his theme, the Jews given the first and ample offer.

Gill: while some were convinced, others were hardened and incredulous — the gospel the savour of life to some, of death to others (2 Corinthians 2:16; as the sun melts wax and hardens clay, the difference in the heart not the message); Barnes: 'hardened' denotes insensibility and opposition, they 'spake evil of that way' publicly (the opposition turning to blasphemy); Paul, when the synagogue grew hostile, withdrew the disciples to the school of Tyrannus (the wisdom of separating from fruitless opposition to a more open field, the ministry continued).

themes: divers were hardened... spake evil of that way
the savour of life and death
withdrawing to the school of Tyrannus

schools.summary: Barnes: the public instruction continued two whole years, so that the great mass of the people in the province of Asia (Ephesus its capital) heard the word — Ephesus a center of commerce and worship with a constant influx of people, the gospel spreading from there throughout the province; the fruit of Paul's patient, prolonged daily ministry (the steady persevering work, not a brief visit, filling the province); 'both Jews and Greeks' the breadth of the reach; the word carried by converts and visitors (perhaps the churches of Asia, Revelation 2-3, beginning).

Gill: not common or vulgar miracles but rare, uncommon ones with a visible display of God's power, ascribed to God as the efficient cause, the apostle only an instrument; Barnes: 'special miracles' = remarkable, uncommon, very unusual; God working extraordinary miracles by Paul's hands (the handkerchiefs healing, v.12) to confirm his doctrine and mission; 'God wrought' — the miracles God's work, Paul the channel (the glory God's, not the instrument's); the true miracles of God against the magic and superstition of Ephesus (vv.13-19).

themes: God wrought special miracles by Paul
God the worker, Paul the instrument

schools.summary: Gill: the handkerchiefs (Paul wiped his face) and aprons (worn at his trade) brought from his body to the sick; Barnes: cloths he had used or touched; the sick healed and the possessed delivered — the power not in the cloth (as a charm or relic) but in God, honouring the faith of the seekers (as the woman touching Christ's garment, Matthew 9:21; Peter's shadow, Acts 5:15); the witnesses careful to ascribe the power to God not the material (against relic-superstition); the special miracles peculiar to the apostolic age, confirming the gospel in a city of magic.

debates.question: Was the power in the cloths themselves?

sides.position: No — the power was God's, honouring the faith of the seekers, not the cloth (against relic-superstition)

themes: handkerchiefs or aprons from his body
the diseases departed, the spirits went out
God honouring faith, not the cloth

schools.summary: Gill: the 'vagabond Jews, exorcists' strolled about pretending to cure diseases by charms and cast out devils by conjuration; seeing the power of the name in Paul's hands, they attempted to use it ('We adjure you by Jesus whom Paul preacheth') as a charm/magic formula, without faith (knowing the name only as Paul's, not their own Lord); the presumption of using the name without belief (the name mighty only to faith, not a magic word); Matthew Henry: the conquest of Satan even in those devoted to him (the false exorcists), the gospel's triumph over Ephesian magic.

themes: we adjure you by Jesus whom Paul preacheth
the name used as a charm

schools.summary: Barnes: Sceva a Greek name, 'chief of the priests' meaning not the high priest (improbable his sons would be wandering exorcists) but of the sacerdotal order, a chief priest of distinction; Gill: they strolled about using exorcisms; the seven sons of a priestly family among the exorcists attempting to use the name of Jesus — the degradation of a priestly family into magic and imposture (the heritage no proof of grace, the priestly descent abused), the presumption of using the name without faith; the false magic-using priesthood contrasted with the true power of God in the apostle.

Barnes paraphrases: 'Jesus I know' (his power to cast out devils), 'Paul I know' (Paul's power), 'but who are ye?' (what power have you? you belong neither to Jesus nor Paul, and have no right to use their name); the demon acknowledging the true power of Jesus and the genuine authority of Paul but defying the impostors (exposing their lack of authority); the exorcists, using the name without faith or authority, exposed by the very demon they sought to expel (the name effectual only to the true servant); the irony of the evil spirit's witness to Jesus and Paul, condemning the false.

themes: Jesus I know, and Paul I know

schools.summary: Clarke marks the wonder — one possessed man more powerful than the seven brothers, stripping and wounding them all (a proof he derived his strength from the evil spirit); Barnes: the extraordinary rage and power of the possessed (Mark 5:3); the demoniac leaping on the exorcists, overcoming them, so they fled naked and wounded — the presumptuous impostors shamefully defeated; the danger of misusing the name without faith or authority (no charm for the unbeliever, the presumption met with defeat); the contrast with Paul (the demon obeying the true servant, overpowering the impostors), the public exposure (v.17).

themes: the man... leaped on them, and overcame them
the danger of misusing the name

schools.summary: Barnes: the transaction showed Paul's miracles in the name of Jesus were real, wrought in attestation of the truth, while the impostors who pretended exposed themselves; 'the name of the Lord Jesus was magnified' = acquired increasing honour; the defeat of the false exorcists becoming known to all (Jews and Greeks), so 'fear fell on them all' (a holy awe and dread of the power of God and of trifling with holy things) and the true name magnified by the exposure of the false; the gospel advanced by the event (leading to the confession and burning of the books, vv.18-19).

themes: the name of the Lord Jesus was magnified
the exposure of the false honouring the true

schools.summary: Barnes: 'their deeds' = their evil course of life, the magical arts they had used, iniquity in general; the lesson — they who truly profess religion will confess their transgressions, and no man has evidence he is renewed who is not willing to confess as well as forsake his sins (Romans 10:10; Proverbs 28:13, 'whoso confesseth and forsaketh them shall have mercy'); the believers openly confessing and showing their deeds (renouncing their magic publicly) — the fruit of true conversion in the open confessing and forsaking of sin, the changed life following the changed heart.

themes: many that believed came, and confessed

schools.summary: Barnes: 'curious arts' (perierga) the arts of magic, jugglery, incantation, much practised at Ephesus (the famed 'Ephesian letters'); the converted magic-users brought their books and burned them publicly, counting the price at fifty thousand pieces of silver (a great sum) — the costly, decisive forsaking of sin (true repentance counting no cost too great, Matthew 5:30); the wisdom of burning rather than selling (not spreading the evil), the thorough destruction; the public testimony to the renunciation and the gospel's triumph over Ephesian magic.

Gill: the word grew and prevailed over conjuring books, conjurers, and even the devils — the power of God going along with it, many converted, delivered from darkness, translated into the kingdom of Christ; Barnes: 'so mightily' = so powerfully, the power making men willing to give up their gains, renounce imposture, and burn their valuable books; the word of God mighty, prevailing over all opposition (magic, greed, demons, deception, 2 Corinthians 10:4); the gospel's triumph in Ephesus (a city of magic and Diana-worship), the Spirit's power making the word effectual.

themes: so mightily grew the word of God

schools.summary: Chrysostom: Paul 'purposed in the Spirit,' showing all he did was done not of his own power; he prophesies 'I must also see Rome' (to comfort and arouse the disciples), and at this point wrote his Epistle to the Corinthians; Paul purposing to pass through Macedonia and Achaia to Jerusalem (with the collection), then to Rome — the divine direction of his plans (whether his own mind resolving or the Spirit directing, under the Spirit's guidance); the 'I must' a divine necessity (his longstanding desire, Romans 1:11-15, the gospel to reach the imperial center, fulfilled by an unexpected way, Acts 28).

themes: the divine direction of his plans
the purpose to reach the imperial city

schools.summary: Barnes: Timothy a proper person to visit the churches (having been there before, Acts 16-17, known and trusted); Erastus the treasurer of Corinth (Romans 16:23), a very proper person for making the collection for the poor at Jerusalem (Paul's wisdom in employing a man accustomed to money matters); Paul sending the two ahead into Macedonia while remaining in Asia — the wise ordering of the mission (matching the man to the task), the delegation to suitable men, the cooperation of the labourers; the apostle continuing his labour while his helpers went ahead.

themes: stayed in Asia for a season
the fit men for the work
the wise ordering of the mission

schools.summary: Gill: 'that way' the Christian religion, the gospel the saints walk in, the stir about how the gospel grew and how many embraced it; Barnes: 'no small stir' = no little excitement, disturbance, tumult; the great Ephesian riot arising from the gospel's success against the worship of Diana (the burning of the books, the turning from idolatry provoking the opposition); the more the gospel prevailed, the more its enemies stirred (1 Corinthians 16:9, the open door and many adversaries); 'the way' the gospel (Acts 9:2; 24:14); the providence overruling the tumult (vv.35-41).

themes: no small stir about that way

schools.summary: Barnes: Demetrius a silversmith making 'silver shrines' (small models or temples of Diana, sold to worshippers and pilgrims), bringing much gain to the craftsmen, his complaint (vv.26-27) showing the trade depended on the worship of Diana; Gill: silver vessels and images; the gospel, turning men from idolatry (v.26), threatened the lucrative trade — the love of money roused (the root of the coming riot, 1 Timothy 6:10, not zeal for Diana but the threatened gain); the avarice cloaked under religious zeal, the conflict of the gospel and the love of money.

themes: Demetrius, a silversmith, made silver shrines for Diana
no small gain unto the craftsmen
the love of money threatened by the gospel
the root of the coming riot

schools.summary: Barnes: Demetrius called together those in his employ and all in the same business (affected the same way, easy to excite), 'by this craft we have our wealth' (our acquisition, property, livelihood); Gill: the chief of the trade, the masters; the frank appeal to self-interest (the love of money the first and frankest argument, stated plainly before the religious pretext, v.27); the craftsmen's opposition springing from their threatened gain (Philippians 2:21); the demagogue's craft (the true motive first, the religious pretext after), the gospel and avarice in conflict.

themes: by this craft we have our wealth
the love of money the first argument

schools.summary: Barnes marks the irony — the noble testimony of a heathen to the zeal and success of Paul's ministry (an acknowledgment that his labours turned away much people from idolatry, not only at Ephesus but throughout all Asia); Demetrius complaining that Paul taught 'they be no gods, which are made with hands' — the enemy unwittingly confessing the gospel's wide triumph (the opposition itself a testimony to its power); the truth against idolatry (idols no gods, 1 Corinthians 8:4), the gospel delivering men from the worship of idols, the truth prevailing.

themes: this Paul hath persuaded and turned away much people
no gods which are made with hands
the enemy testifying to the gospel's success

schools.summary: Gill: the craft brought into contempt if men believed the images had no divinity, and the temple and worship of Diana despised; Demetrius, having appealed to gain (vv.25-26), now cloaks the threat under concern for the temple and honour of 'the great goddess Diana... whom all Asia and the world worshippeth' (her temple one of the wonders of the world) — the religious pretext masking the love of money (the avarice clothed in zeal), the appeal to civic and religious pride; the irony — a god that can be 'destroyed' by men turning away is no god, the fear exposing the idol's vanity.

themes: our craft in danger... the temple of Diana despised
whom all Asia and the world worshippeth

schools.summary: Barnes: they were full of wrath (probably at losing their gains); 'great' often applied to Diana by the Greeks; the design of the clamour to raise a persecution against Paul and secure their employment; 'often, when men have no arguments, they raise a clamour' (when their employments are in danger, they are filled with rage); the cry 'Great is Diana' the religious banner under which the love of money marched (the avarice clamouring under a pretext), the mob roused by passion not argument; the irony — the loud cry asserting the idol's greatness against the truth that exposed it.

themes: full of wrath, and cried out
Great is Diana of the Ephesians
the clamour of greed cloaked in zeal
the mob roused when arguments fail

schools.summary: Gill: the workmen ran through the city crying out, bringing the people out, the mob gathering and increasing, throwing the city into confusion; Barnes identifies Gaius (who entertained Paul, 1 Corinthians 1:14) and Aristarchus (who later attended Paul to Rome, Colossians 4:10); the whole city filled with confusion, the mob seizing Paul's companions and rushing into the theatre (the great public place) — the tumult spreading, the innocent companions seized (suffering for their association with Paul, the cost of fellowship with the persecuted), the danger preserved by providence (vv.35-41).

themes: the whole city filled with confusion

schools.summary: Gill: Paul would have entered the theatre to preach to the people (to remove their prejudices, show the error of their idolatry, reconcile them) and to defend his companions, risking his life — showing his greatness of soul, firmness, intrepidity, and concern for his companions; Barnes: he wished to address them and defend his cause; the disciples 'suffered him not' (preventing him from exposing himself to the mob's fury) — the wise care restraining his courage; the balance of courage and prudence (zeal tempered by discretion), the providence preserving the apostle's life through the disciples' care.

themes: Paul would have entered in unto the people

schools.summary: Gill identifies 'the chief of Asia' as the Asiarchs — not princes or civil magistrates but officers who presided over the public games and diversions at the theatre, men of rank and influence, some of whom were Paul's friends; even some of these high officers (presiding over the very theatre) being Paul's friends, sent to urge him not to venture in — the friendship and respect Paul won even among the great (the gospel commending him to men of rank), their care for his safety; the providence raising up friends in high places, the apostle preserved by their counsel.

themes: the chief of Asia, which were his friends
desiring him not to adventure into the theatre

schools.summary: Barnes: an admirable description of a mob, assembled for what purpose they knew not, but agitated by passions, strifes, and tumults; the greater part did not even know why they had come together; the disorderly assembly (some crying one thing, some another, no unity or reason, only noise) — the picture of a mob moved by passion not reason, the people swept along ignorant of the cause; the contrast with the reasoned, persuasive gospel (v.8); the providence in the confusion (the riot, having no real cause or unity, the more easily quieted, v.35).

themes: some cried one thing, and some another
most knew not why they came

schools.summary: Gill: some think this Alexander the coppersmith, who later did Paul much evil (2 Timothy 4:14); a Jew, put forward by the Jews — the Jews (also enemies of idolatry, liable to the mob's rage) wishing to distance themselves from Paul and the Christians, putting Alexander forward to make a defence (to clear the Jews of the charge, show they were not the same as the Christians); the self-protective move (caring for their own safety, not the gospel); the attempt futile (the mob, perceiving he was a Jew, would not hear him, v.34), the providence in the added confusion.

themes: they drew Alexander, the Jews putting him forward

schools.summary: Barnes: a general prejudice against the Jews, the mob (perceiving Alexander a Jew) unwilling to hear any defence; 'with one voice' (unitedly, one continued shout) 'about the space of two hours' they cried out; Gill: they knew the Jew equally an enemy to their idolatry; the mob crying 'Great is Diana' for two hours without reason or argument — the unreasoning frenzy and irrational persistence of passion (zeal without knowledge, the frenzy in defence of a vanity, an idol that was no god); the providence in the spent frenzy preparing for the quieting (v.35).

themes: all with one voice about two hours cried out
Great is Diana of the Ephesians
the unreasoning frenzy of the mob

schools.summary: Gill: the 'townclerk' more than a common clerk — a chief man of the city, a recorder/registrar of high office, a man of authority and influence; he 'appeased the people' (by his authority and prudence quieting the mob so he could be heard); his prudent opening — acknowledging the well-known devotion of Ephesus to 'the great goddess Diana, and the image which fell down from Jupiter' (the Ephesians' belief the image fell from heaven), calming them by granting their zeal and assuring their worship secure, that he might lead them to reason; the wisdom that conciliates before it corrects.

themes: the city a worshipper of Diana
quieting the mob by acknowledging their zeal

schools.summary: Gill: the officer intimates the city's devotion to Diana so certain and well known that nobody would contradict it (the worship secure), therefore they were mistaken in their alarm and ought to be quiet; Barnes: since the zeal of the Ephesians for Diana could not be doubted, there was no danger, and they should do nothing rashly; the town clerk removing the ground of the mob's fear (their worship not in real danger) and warning against rash action ('do nothing rashly') — the call to calm and deliberation, the prudent leading from passion to reason (contrasting Demetrius's inflaming).

themes: these things cannot be spoken against
ye ought to be quiet, do nothing rashly

schools.summary: Barnes: the Greek (hierosylous) means not 'robbers of churches' (no Christian churches yet built) but those who commit sacrilege, plundering temples; the town clerk testifies the men (Gaius and Aristarchus) had neither robbed the temple of Diana nor blasphemed the goddess — a heathen official vindicating the Christians' innocence (their conduct blameless even in the eyes of their accusers); Paul preached the truth (idols no gods, v.26) positively without robbing temples or railing at Diana (the wisdom of confessing the truth without needless offence, 1 Peter 2:12), the blameless life a witness.

themes: neither robbers of churches, nor blasphemers of your goddess
the truth preached without needless offence

schools.summary: Barnes: 'the law is open' = there are court days appointed for judicial trials; 'deputies' = the Roman proconsuls (Acts 13:7), the cause to be brought before them and decided properly; the town clerk directs Demetrius and the craftsmen to the lawful courts ('let them implead one another') — if they have a complaint, let them bring it lawfully, not by mob violence; the appeal to law against the riot (the rule of law, grievances redressed by law not violence, 1 Corinthians 14:40); the prudent redirection from violence to law, implying the mob's violence unjustified and no lawful charge made (the apostles' innocence, v.37).

themes: the law is open, and there are deputies
the appeal to law against the mob

schools.summary: Barnes: 'in a lawful assembly' = an assembly convened in conformity to law, not by tumult and riot; the present a tumultuous assemblage, proper for the officer to demand it disperse, public grievances addressed in a lawful not riotous assembly; the town clerk directing that any public matter be settled in a lawful, regularly convened assembly — the upholding of lawful order against the riotous mob (the order of law against the disorder of the riot, 1 Corinthians 14:40), removing all ground for the tumult by pointing to lawful remedies (vv.38-39), the riot rebuked as unlawful.

themes: concerning other matters... a lawful assembly
the rule of law against the mob

schools.summary: Gill: liable to be called to account, reproved, and punished by the Roman proconsul or emperor for this day's uproar (capable of being interpreted as a riot and sedition); Barnes: such a tumult would attract the magistrates' displeasure, popular commotions justly dreaded by the Roman government; the town clerk warning the city in danger for the riot, 'there being no cause' to justify the concourse (indefensible before Rome) — quieting the mob by the fear of Roman displeasure (the dread of the penalty for sedition); the providence using the Roman order to protect the apostles and the church.

themes: in danger to be called in question for this uproar
the providence protecting through civil order

schools.summary: Clarke: from this prudent man's conduct we learn how much influence wise words have ('how forcible are right words!' Job 6:25), the riot quieted by one wise man's speech; the word 'assembly' (ekklesia, usually 'church') here signifies an assembly of any kind, good or bad, legal or illegal; Gill: he dismissed them after his oration; the providence delivering the apostles and the church through the wisdom of a heathen official, the order of Roman law, and the fear of Rome (2 Peter 2:9, the Lord delivering the godly); the close of the great Ephesian tumult, Paul soon to depart (Acts 20:1).
